title: Exploring taxa distribution across space and time
text: get_datasets() extends plan Datasets table — central organising unit VegVault. dataset unique ID, geographic coordinates (coord_long, coord_lat), dataset type (e.g. vegetation_plot), source type (e.g. BIEN, sPlotOpen, Neotoma-FOSSILPOL), sampling method. point every dataset database scope — subsequent steps narrow selection.
code: plan <-   plan |>   get_datasets()

title: Exploring taxa distribution across space and time
text: VegVault contains four dataset types: vegetation_plot — contemporary vegetation surveys (BIEN, sPlotOpen) fossil_pollen_archive — sediment-core pollen records (Neotoma-FOSSILPOL) traits — functional trait measurements (TRY) gridpoints — artificially created datasets holding abiotic (climate/soil) data, spatially linked three types full technical description dataset type, see Datasets section VegVault Database Structure. select_dataset_by_type() adds type filter plan. keep two types carry occurrence information.
code: plan <-   plan |>   select_dataset_by_type(     sel_dataset_type = c(       "vegetation_plot",       "fossil_pollen_archive"     )   )

title: Exploring taxa distribution across space and time
text: extend plan geographic filter: bounding box Pacific Northwest (longitude −116 −114 °W, latitude 44 46 °N), area captures BIEN vegetation plots Neotoma-FOSSILPOL pollen cores. filter pushed SQL engine — data transferred yet.
code: plan <-   plan |>   select_dataset_by_geo(     long_lim = c(-116, -114),     lat_lim  = c(44, 46)   ) #> The data does not contain the all dataset types specified in `vec_dataset_type`.  #>  Changing `vec_dataset_type` to the dataset types present in the data as: vegetation_plot, fossil_pollen_archive

title: Exploring taxa distribution across space and time
text: get_taxa() extends plan taxonomic occurrence data, including abundance values (value). Raw taxon names VegVault come directly primary sources may differ databases (e.g. Betula pendula vegetation plot vs. Betula pollen record). classify_to = "genus" argument uses pre-computed TaxonClassification table — built {taxospace} package using GBIF Taxonomy Backbone — roll identifications genus level, making vegetation plot records fossil pollen records directly comparable. title: Combining vegetation data with climate variables
text: Species distribution models (SDMs) require paired observations species occurrences environmental predictors locations. VegVault stores modern vegetation_plot records climate gridpoints single database. get_abiotic_data() function automatically links vegetation sample spatially temporally proximate gridpoint samples, never manage spatial join . core idea vaultkeepr build plan first, execute . function call — get_datasets(), select_dataset_by_type(), get_samples(), — adds step lazy SQL query stored plan object without touching database. plan fully specified, single call extract_data() executes complete query returns tibble ready analysis. Abiotic data VegVault come CHELSA (high-resolution climate), CHELSA-TRACE21 (palaeoclimate back 21 ka), WoSIS (soil properties). Variable names follow original source conventions: CHELSA bioclimatic variables named bio1–bio19, WoSIS soil classification HWSD2. full description VegVault documentation. article prepare dataset SDM Europe: extract modern (age = 0) vegetation plots together mean annual air temperature (bio1, °C) nearest gridpoints. use small example database (built automatically ) mirrors naming conventions real VegVault file. title: Combining vegetation data with climate variables
text: data_eu_sdm holds one row per dataset. Vegetation plot datasets carry taxon abundances data_community (wide format: one column per taxon) linked climate values data_abiotic. unnest compute per-sample species richness pair sample mean annual air temperature.
code: # Species richness per sample from the nested community data data_richness <-   data_eu_sdm |>   dplyr::filter(dataset_type == "vegetation_plot") |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, data_community) |>   tidyr::unnest(cols = data_community) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(     cols = -c(dataset_name, sample_name),     names_to = "taxon",     values_to = "abund"   ) |>   dplyr::filter(abund > 0) |>   dplyr::group_by(dataset_name, sample_name) |>   dplyr::summarise(     n_taxa = dplyr::n_distinct(taxon),     .groups = "drop"   )  # Temperature (bio1) per sample from the nested abiotic data data_temp <-   data_eu_sdm |>   dplyr::filter(dataset_type == "vegetation_plot") |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, data_abiotic) |>   tidyr::unnest(cols = data_abiotic) |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, sample_name, abiotic_value) |>   dplyr::distinct()  # Join richness to temperature data_for_plot <-   data_richness |>   dplyr::left_join(     data_temp,     by = dplyr::join_by(dataset_name, sample_name)   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na(abiotic_value)  ggplot2::ggplot(   data = data_for_plot,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(x = abiotic_value, y = n_taxa) ) +   ggplot2::geom_point(     colour = col_blue_light,     alpha  = 0.7,     size   = 2   ) +   ggplot2::geom_smooth(     method  = "lm",     formula = y ~ x,     colour  = col_brown_dark,     se      = FALSE   ) +   ggplot2::labs(     title   = "Species richness vs. mean annual air temperature (bio1)",     x       = "Mean annual air temperature (bio1, \u00b0C)",     y       = "Number of taxa per vegetation plot",     caption = "Based on the vaultkeepr example database (CHELSA bio1)"   )

title: Reconstructing functional diversity through time
text: Paleoecological records fossil pollen archives capture plant communities shifted response past climate change. linked trait databases, records can reveal changes functional composition time — instance, whether communities became taller acquisitive warmer periods. Trait data VegVault follow Diaz et al. (2016) framework sourced TRY BIEN. Six trait domains included: Stem specific density, Leaf nitrogen content per unit mass, Diaspore mass, Plant heigh, Leaf Area, Leaf mass per area. Within domain, individual trait names preserve original name primary source (e.g. "whole plant height" BIEN, "Plant height vegetative" TRY). article combine fossil pollen archives plant height data South America, covering 4 000–8 000 cal yr BP window, visualise community-weighted mean height changes across time. use small example database (built automatically ) mirrors structure naming conventions real VegVault file. title: Reconstructing functional diversity through time
text: data_sa_traits holds one row per dataset. Fossil pollen datasets carry pollen abundances data_community (wide format: one column per genus) sample ages data_samples. Trait datasets carry trait observations data_traits (wide format: one row per trait × sample, one column per genus). unnest compute community-weighted mean (CWM) plant height every fossil pollen sample (1) extracting mean trait value per genus data_traits, (2) computing abundance-weighted mean genera present fossil sample.
code: # Mean trait value per genus from the nested trait datasets data_genus_trait <-   data_sa_traits |>   dplyr::filter(dataset_type == "traits") |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, data_traits) |>   tidyr::unnest(cols = data_traits) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(     cols = -c(dataset_name, sample_name, trait_domain_name, trait_name),     names_to = "taxon_name",     values_to = "trait_value"   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na(trait_value) |>   dplyr::group_by(taxon_name) |>   dplyr::summarise(     mean_trait_value = mean(trait_value, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  # Sample ages from fossil pollen datasets data_ages <-   data_sa_traits |>   dplyr::filter(dataset_type == "fossil_pollen_archive") |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, data_samples) |>   tidyr::unnest(cols = data_samples) |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, sample_name, age)  # Fossil pollen occurrences (long format) with ages data_fossil_occ <-   data_sa_traits |>   dplyr::filter(dataset_type == "fossil_pollen_archive") |>   dplyr::select(dataset_name, data_community) |>   tidyr::unnest(cols = data_community) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(     cols = -c(dataset_name, sample_name),     names_to = "taxon_name",     values_to = "abund"   ) |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(abund), abund > 0) |>   dplyr::left_join(     data_ages,     by = dplyr::join_by(dataset_name, sample_name)   )  # CWM per fossil sample: sum(abundance * trait) / sum(abundance) data_cwm <-   data_fossil_occ |>   dplyr::left_join(     data_genus_trait,     by = dplyr::join_by(taxon_name)   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na(mean_trait_value) |>   dplyr::group_by(dataset_name, sample_name, age) |>   dplyr::summarise(     cwm = sum(abund * mean_trait_value) / sum(abund),     .groups = "drop"   )  data_for_plot <-   data_cwm |>   dplyr::mutate(     age_bin = cut(       age,       breaks = c(4000, 6000, 8000),       labels = c("4 000–6 000 BP", "6 000–8 000 BP"),       include.lowest = TRUE     )   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na(age_bin)  ggplot2::ggplot(   data = data_for_plot,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(x = age_bin, y = cwm) ) +   ggplot2::geom_violin(     fill   = col_blue_light,     colour = col_green_dark,     width  = 0.5   ) +   ggplot2::labs(     title   = "CWM plant height through time",     x       = "Age bin",     y       = "Community-weighted mean plant height",     caption = "Based on the vaultkeepr example database"   )

title: Understanding paleoecological uncertainty
text: Paleoecological records fossil pollen archives inherently uncertain two distinct ways. Age uncertainty arises age sediment layer measured directly — estimated age-depth model (e.g. Bchron, OxCal) fits curve small number radiocarbon dates extrapolates . Running model many times using Bayesian Monte Carlo framework produces distribution plausible ages every sample depth — stored VegVault iterations. Per-sample richness (fixed across iterations: a sample's taxa never change) data_richness_per_sample <-   data_sample_taxa |>   dplyr::group_by(.data$sample_name) |>   dplyr::summarise(     n_taxa = dplyr::n_distinct(.data$taxon_name),     .groups = "drop"   )  # ",       "Based on the vaultkeepr example database; all values are synthetic."     )   )

Per-sample richness (fixed across iterations: a sample's taxa never change) data_richness_per_sample <-   data_sample_taxa |>   dplyr::group_by(.data$sample_name) |>   dplyr::summarise(     n_taxa = dplyr::n_distinct(.data$taxon_name),     .groups = "drop"   )  # Attach per-iteration ages: each sample gets one row per iteration data_richness_iter <-   data_richness_per_sample |>   dplyr::inner_join(data_age_unc_long, by = "sample_name") |>   dplyr::arrange(.data$iteration, .data$age_uncertainty)  # Consensus line: each sample placed at its median age across iterations data_richness_median <-   data_richness_iter |>   dplyr::group_by(.data$sample_name, .data$n_taxa) |>   dplyr::summarise(     age_uncertainty = stats::median(.data$age_uncertainty),     .groups = "drop"   ) |>   dplyr::arrange(.data$age_uncertainty)  ggplot2::ggplot(   data = data_richness_iter,   mapping = ggplot2::aes(     x = age_uncertainty,     y = n_taxa,     group = as.factor(.data$iteration)   ) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(     colour = col_blue_light,     alpha = 0.4,     linewidth = 0.5   ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(     data = data_richness_median,     mapping = ggplot2::aes(group = NULL),     colour = col_green_dark,     linewidth = 1.2   ) +   ggplot2::scale_x_reverse() +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Genus richness per sample across age-model iterations",     x = "Age (cal yr BP)",     y = "Genus richness (per sample)",     caption = paste0(       "Thin lines = individual age-model iterations (each sample at its ",       "iteration-specific age, connected in age order).\n",       "Thick line = median-age consensus. ",       "Based on the vaultkeepr example database; all values are synthetic."     )   )

title: Understanding paleoecological uncertainty
text: Build plan retrieve age uncertainty single script:
code: plan <-   open_vault(path = path_to_db) |>   get_datasets() |>   select_dataset_by_type(     sel_dataset_type = "fossil_pollen_archive"   ) |>   select_dataset_by_geo(     long_lim = c(-116, -114),     lat_lim  = c(44, 46)   ) |>   get_samples() |>   select_samples_by_age(age_lim = c(0, 8000))  # Age uncertainty — default wide output (sample_name + one col per iteration) data_age_unc <-   get_age_uncertainty(con = plan)  # Extend with taxa for the richness-per-iteration analysis # default packed output; unnested inside the analysis chunk below data_taxa_packed <-   plan |>   get_taxa(classify_to = "genus") |>   extract_data(verbose = FALSE)
